title: Epidural Hematoma
chief_complaint: My friend hit his head in a skateboarding accident 4 hours ago. He was knocked out for 2 minutes, woke up and seemed fine, but now he's getting drowsy and confused. His right pupil is bigger than the left.

physical_exam.Neurologic: GCS 9 (E2 V2 M5 - deteriorating). Right pupil 6mm, fixed (uncal herniation compressing CN III). Left pupil 3mm, reactive. Right hemiparesis (ipsilateral to blown pupil in 85% of epidural hematomas - false localizing sign from Kernohan notch phenomenon).
physical_exam.Head: Left temporal scalp hematoma and laceration. Battle sign absent (mastoid ecchymosis - basilar skull fracture).
physical_exam.Cushing: Cushing triad: HTN, Bradycardia, Irregular respirations (increased ICP)

hidden_data.ct_head: Biconvex (lenticular) hyperdense lesion in left temporal region (60mL). Midline shift 8mm to right. Uncal herniation. Left lateral ventricle compressed. Skull fracture through left temporal bone crossing middle meningeal artery groove.

correct_diagnosis: Acute Epidural Hematoma (Left Temporal) from Middle Meningeal Artery Laceration with Uncal Herniation
key_learning_points: Epidural hematoma = arterial bleeding between skull and dura. Most common source: Middle meningeal artery (temporal bone fracture). Classic lucid interval: Loss of consciousness → Awake and alert → Rapid deterioration (hours)

CT: Biconvex (lenticular) hyperdensity. Does NOT cross suture lines (dura tightly adherent at sutures). Subdural hematoma: Crescent-shaped, crosses sutures, venous bleeding, more common in elderly/anticoagulated
Cushing triad (HTN, Bradycardia, Irregular breathing) = sign of increased ICP and impending herniation. Blown pupil (CN III compression) = ipsilateral to lesion in 85%

Treatment: EMERGENCY CRANIOTOMY AND EVACUATION. Burr hole if no neurosurgeon available (temporizing measure). Mannitol 1g/kg IV or Hypertonic saline 3% 250mL for temporizing. Hyperventilate to PaCO2 30-35 (short-term, reduces ICP). Elevate head of bed 30°. Reverse anticoagulation if applicable
Prognosis: Mortality 5-10% if treated promptly. Excellent prognosis if awake at time of surgery. Coma at presentation = 40% mortality. Key: Early recognition and rapid surgical decompression
